# crimes — codebase risk workflow

`crimes` is a deterministic CLI (no LLM) that reports change risk and
agent risk. JSON output is the stable contract; prefer it when planning.

## When to run it

- Before editing an unfamiliar file: `crimes context <file> --format json`
- Before a broad refactor: `crimes scan <path> --format json`
- After edits: `crimes scan --changed --format json`
- Before merging a branch: `crimes verdict --format json`

## Decision rules

- Treat any new `severity: "high"` finding introduced by your edit as a blocker unless the user explicitly accepts it.
- Read `evidence[]` before acting; it contains deterministic facts, not LLM opinion.
- Use `scores.agent_risk` to decide which findings need human attention first.
- If a finding is a false positive, record feedback with `crimes feedback <fingerprint> --verdict fp --note "<why>"` rather than silently ignoring it.

Assert observable output, emitted files,\\n  errors, or CLI results.\\n\\n## Agent Safety Rules\\n\\n- Do not publish packages.\\n- Do not run `npm publish`, `pnpm publish`, `git push`, or create tags unless\\n  explicitly requested.\\n- Treat `src/index.ts`, `src/esbuild/index.ts`, `src/plugin.ts`, and\\n  `src/utils.ts` as load-bearing. Read callers/importers before changing them.\\n- Prefer additive, narrowly scoped edits over broad rewrites.\\n```\\n\\nThat gives agents the minimum useful contract: how to set up, how to verify, where responsibilities live, and what operations are unsafe.\"}}\n{\"type\":\"turn.completed\",\"usage\":{\"input_tokens\":42264,\"cached_input_tokens\":12160,\"output_tokens\":639,\"reasoning_output_tokens\":29}}\n",

/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.8.1/claude/refactor-04-monorepo.json:7:  "response": "# Refactor plan — petty agent-risk in `src/index.ts`, `src/rollup.ts`, `src/esbuild/svelte.ts`\n\n## Findings in scope (8)\n\n**False Identity — `name_behavior_mismatch`**\n- `crime_00030` — `src/index.ts:167` `build` — name reads as pure transform, body calls `createLogger`, `removeFiles` (4 side-effect signals)\n- `crime_00057` — `src/index.ts:291` `buildAll` — calls `removeFiles` (3 signals)\n- `crime_00052` — `src/rollup.ts:38` `getRollupConfig` — `get*` reads as a pure accessor, body calls `setSilent`, `removeFiles` (4 signals)\n- `crime_00079` — `src/rollup.ts:77` `buildStart` — calls `removeFiles` (2 signals)\n\n**Option Bag Junk Drawer — `option_bag_junk_drawer`**\n- `crime_00056` — `src/rollup.ts:38` `getRollupConfig(options)` — 10 distinct property reads off a generic bag\n\n**Return Shape Roulette — `return_shape_roulette`**\n- `crime_00054` — `src/esbuild/svelte.ts:9` `sveltePlugin` — 6 object-literal return shapes, 0% key overlap\n- `crime_00055` — `src/esbuild/svelte.ts:17` `setup` — 6 shapes, 0% overlap\n- `crime_00062` — `src/esbuild/svelte.ts:31` anonymous handler — 5 shapes, 0% overlap\n\n---\n\n## What an agent can get wrong today\n\n### `src/rollup.ts` — `getRollupConfig`\nA `get*` name promises a read. Reality: it mutates the logger via `setSilent` *and* deletes the output directory via `removeFiles`. An agent looking to \"cache the config for watch mode\" or \"precompute config once at startup\" will silently double-delete `outDir` and stomp on logger state on the second call. The `options` bag amplifies the trap — the agent reading the signature sees `(options)` and has no way to know which of the 10 fields (`clean`, `outDir`, `silent`, …) drive the destructive paths.\n\n### `src/index.ts` — `build` / `buildAll`\nBoth are top-of-the-file orchestrators with safe-sounding names. An agent wrapping them in `Promise.all`, retry-with-backoff, or \"rebuild on file change\" assumes idempotence; the calls to `removeFiles` make them anything but. `build` is also the 293-line god function, so the side effects are buried far below the signature where an agent skimming the head of the file won't see them.\n\n### `src/esbuild/svelte.ts` — return shapes\nThe three plugin callbacks return six distinct object shapes with **zero key overlap** between branches: `{ path, namespace }` for resolve hits, `{ contents, loader }` for loads, `{ location, text }` for diagnostics, etc. Today they flow through inferred `any`. An agent adding a 7th branch (say, a watch-mode invalidation) has no type-driven signal for:\n- Which keys esbuild actually consumes in each callback context (esbuild silently ignores keys outside the expected shape).\n- Whether `errors` can be mixed with `{ contents, loader }` (it can — but the agent has to read the esbuild docs to discover that).\n- Which of the six existing shapes is the template for their new branch.\n\n---\n\n## Proposed refactor\n\n### Step 1 — type the option bag (`crime_00056`)\n\n`getRollupConfig` reads 10 fields. Introduce a `Pick`-derived type so the parameter is a checklist, not a junk drawer:\n\n```ts\ntype RollupConfigInput = Pick<NormalizedOptions,\n  | 'entry' | 'external' | 'format' | 'outDir' | 'outExtension'\n  | 'cjsInterop' | 'clean' | 'dts' | 'silent' /* … */>;\n```\n\n`Pick<NormalizedOptions, …>` beats a brand-new interface here: the canonical shape already exists upstream, so duplicating it would drift. Adding a new field becomes a deliberate edit to the `Pick` keys before the function can read it.\n\nZero behaviour change. Required precondition for steps 2 and 3 because once the type narrows, the side-effect extraction below has a clean signature to hand off to.\n\n### Step 2 — extract the side effects, then rename (`crime_00030/52/57/79`)\n\nDon't just rename — **split the pure read from the mutation**, then rename what's left:\n\n```ts\n// src/rollup.ts — before\nexport const getRollupConfig = async (options) => {\n  setSilent(options.silent)\n  await removeFiles(options.clean, options.outDir)\n  return { /* config */ }\n}\n\n// after\nconst buildRollupConfig = (options: RollupConfigInput): RollupConfig => { /* pure */ }\n\nconst prepareRollupOutput = async (options: RollupConfigInput): Promise<void> => {\n  setSilent(options.silent)\n  if (options.clean) await removeFiles(options.clean, options.outDir)\n}\n```\n\nRename map for the others:\n| Before | After | Notes |\n|---|---|---|\n| `build` (index.ts:167) | `runBuild` + `setupRunArtifacts(options)` | `run*` discloses effects; helper extracts the `createLogger` / `removeFiles` calls |\n| `buildAll` (index.ts:291) | `runAllBuilds` + `cleanBuildOutputs(options)` | Caller invokes the clean step explicitly |\n| `buildStart` (rollup.ts:77) | **keep** | Rollup plugin lifecycle hook — renaming breaks Rollup's contract. Add a one-line `// rollup hook — clears prior dist on each rebuild` comment instead |\n\nRule for the future: any function whose first call is `removeFiles` must either (a) say so in its name (`clean*`, `reset*`, `prepare*`) or (b) be a framework-required hook with a one-line comment.\n\n### Step 3 — name the return shapes (`crime_00054/55/62`)\n\nThe 6 distinct shapes map to **3 esbuild callback contracts**. Name them and let the union do the work:\n\n```ts\ntype SvelteResolveResult  = { path: string; namespace?: string; }\ntype SvelteLoadResult     = { contents: string; loader?: esbuild.Loader; }\ntype SvelteDiagnostic     = { errors?: PartialMessage[]; warnings?: PartialMessage[]; }\n\nbuild.onResolve({ filter }, (args): SvelteResolveResult | SvelteDiagnostic => { /* … */ })\nbuild.onLoad({ filter },    (args): SvelteLoadResult    | SvelteDiagnostic => { /* … */ })\n```\n\nSix freelance shapes collapse into three named contracts that match esbuild's own callback typing. An agent adding a 7th branch now gets a TS error if they mix keys across the union — the bug becomes a compile failure instead of a silently-ignored field. For an **esbuild plugin regression** specifically, one finding jumps out:\n\n**`crime_00024` — `test/index.test.ts` lines 537–548**\n\n> - \"test: \\\"native-node-module plugin should handle *.node(.js) import properly\\\"\"\n> - \"0 expect/assert calls\"\n> - \"lines 537-548\"\n\nI'd strengthen this one first. This one literally says `\"native-node-module plugin should handle *.node(.js) import properly\"`.\n2. **It has zero assertions, not just weak ones** (confidence `0.88`). The test name makes a behavioural promise — \"should handle … properly\" — and then the body proves nothing. CI will stay green as long as the bundle doesn't throw, even if the plugin silently stops handling `*.node`/`*.node.js` imports correctly. That is precisely the failure mode the user described.\n\n**The assertion I'd add:** read the bundled output and assert that `*.node` imports survive as runtime `require()` calls instead of being inlined or stripped. Concretely, something like:\n\n```ts\nconst contents = await getFileContents(outputFiles, 'index.js')\nexpect(contents).toMatch(/require\\(['\"].+\\.node['\"]\\)/)\nexpect(contents).not.toContain('Cannot find module')\n```\n\nThe exact regex depends on what the native-node-module plugin is supposed to emit, but the principle is: the test's name promises the plugin handles `*.node(.js)` imports — so the assertion must inspect the output and confirm the import was handled in the expected shape. Without that, the test only proves the build didn't crash, which is exactly the gap the regression exploited.",

(Other date-related findings — `direct_date` crime_00002, `mixed_utc_local_methods` crime_00003, `timezone_unsafe_parse` crime_00007, `dst_naive_arithmetic` crime_00011, `locale_drift` crime_00020 — are different charges and out of scope here, but several touch the same file, see \"Related\" below.)\n\n## The line in question\n\n`src/date.ts:39–42` — the function whose body L41 lives in:\n\n```ts\nexport function dateLabel(): string {\n  const d = new Date();\n  return d.getUTCFullYear() + \"-\" + d.getMonth() + \"-\" + d.getDate();\n}\n```\n\nThree bugs on one line: `+`-concatenation (the flagged crime), `getMonth()` is 0-indexed and unpadded, mixed UTC reads with local reads (crime_00003 covers the UTC/local mix on the same receiver).\n\n## Proposed replacement\n\nThe choice between `toISOString()` and `Intl.DateTimeFormat` is **driven by what callers do with `dateLabel()`'s return value**:\n\n| Caller use | Choice | Replacement |\n|---|---|---|\n| Persistence / cache keys / log lines / filenames / IDs | `toISOString()` | `` return new Date().toISOString().slice(0, 10); // \"YYYY-MM-DD\" `` |\n| Rendered to a human in the UI | `Intl.DateTimeFormat` | `` return new Intl.DateTimeFormat(\"en-CA\", { timeZone: \"UTC\", year: \"numeric\", month: \"2-digit\", day: \"2-digit\" }).format(new Date()); `` |\n\n**Recommended default for this symbol: `toISOString().slice(0, 10)`.** Two reasons:\n1. The function name is `dateLabel` but the existing output shape (`YYYY-M-D`, ASCII-only, no localisation) reads as a machine key, not a rendered label. The crime_00020 `toLocaleDateString()` at L46 (`prettyDueDate`) is the file's actual user-facing renderer.\n2. The existing reads were already in the UTC family (`getUTCFullYear()`), so an ISO UTC date string is the smallest-semantic-delta fix.\n\nBefore committing, **grep call sites of `dateLabel`** and confirm — if any call site puts the return value into a `<span>` / toast / email body, switch that call site to `Intl.DateTimeFormat` instead.\n\n## Patch (minimal)\n\n```ts\nexport function dateLabel(): string {\n  return new Date().toISOString().slice(0, 10);\n}\n```\n\nThis single edit clears `crime_00019` and also resolves `crime_00003` (\"Half-UTC, Half-Local\") on the same receiver, because the new body no longer reads the `d` Date at all — the UTC/local mix vanishes with the concatenation.\n\n## Related crimes worth bundling\n\nWhile editing `src/date.ts`, the following are cheap to fix in the same commit and share the \"standardise temporal output\" theme (but are **not** `date_string_concat` findings — listing for awareness only):\n\n- `crime_00003` *Half-UTC, Half-Local* @L9, L41 — auto-resolved by the patch above.\n- `crime_00020` *Host-Locale Drift* @L46 — `due.toLocaleDateString()` needs an explicit BCP-47 locale or a move to `Intl.DateTimeFormat`. This is the canonical user-facing case in the file.\n- `todayKey()` @L9 uses `` `${d.getUTCFullYear()}-${d.getUTCMonth() + 1}-${d.getUTCDate()}` `` — the detector didn't flag it (template literal, not `+`-concat), but it is the same anti-pattern. If you want a clean sweep, replace with `new Date().toISOString().slice(0, 10)` — `todayKey` is clearly a machine key.\n\n## Rollout\n\n1. Grep `dateLabel(` call sites; confirm none render to a human. If any do, switch that site to `Intl.DateTimeFormat`.\n2. Apply the one-line patch above.\n3. Re-run `crimes scan --format json` and confirm `crime_00019` is gone and `summary.total` drops accordingly. (Expect `crime_00003` to also disappear; `crime_00020` and the L9 template-literal will remain unless bundled.)\n4. Add a test pinning `Date` to a known instant and asserting the `YYYY-MM-DD` shape, so any future regression to manual concatenation is caught.",

/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.8/claude/context-04-monorepo.json:7:  "response": "## Finding: Missing Agent Context (`crime_00009`)\n\nThe scan confirms this repo ships **no agent-readable instruction files**:\n\n- No `AGENTS.md` at the root\n- No `CLAUDE.md` at the root\n- No `.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md`\n- `package.json` declares bins (`tsup`, `tsup-node`) that an agent has no documented way to discover\n\nSeverity: **medium**, confidence: **0.9**, agent_risk: **0.61**.\n\n## Consequence for future agent work\n\nAn agent landing in this repo cold has to *infer* everything — what commands exist, how to run the build/test loop, which files are load-bearing (`src/plugin.ts` fan-in 10, `src/utils.ts` fan-in 18, `src/log.ts` fan-in 9), and which conventions matter. The scan already flags several **God Functions** (`build` 293 LOC, `runEsbuild` 237 LOC, `main` 153 LOC) and **load-bearing hubs**; without context, an agent is likely to:\n\n1. Refactor a God Function without realizing it sits behind 17 fan-out edges\n2. Run the wrong command (or invent one) because `tsup` / `tsup-node` aren't documented\n3. Miss the test convention — and there are 12+ tests in the codebase with zero assertions that look \"real\"\n4. Edit `src/utils.ts` or `src/plugin.ts` (top connectivity tier) as if they were leaf modules\n\nThe finding is medium-severity because the cost is *agent confidence × blast radius*, not a direct bug.\n\n## Minimal `AGENTS.md` skeleton (clears the finding)\n\n```markdown\n# AGENTS.md\n\nThis repo is `tsup` — a TypeScript bundler. Agents should read this\nbefore editing.\n\n## Commands\n\n- `pnpm install` — install deps (pnpm workspaces)\n- `pnpm build` — build via the project's own `tsup` binary\n- `pnpm test` — run the Vitest suite in `test/`\n- `pnpm lint` — Biome\n- `pnpm typecheck` — `tsc --noEmit`\n\nThe published binaries are `tsup` and `tsup-node` (see `package.json` →\n`bin`). Both resolve to the same entry; `tsup-node` runs under Node\nwithout esbuild's child process.\n\n## Load-bearing files (treat as additive-change zones)\n\n- `src/plugin.ts` — 10 importers; the plugin contract\n- `src/utils.ts` — 18 importers; broad helper surface\n- `src/log.ts` — 9 importers; shared logger\n- `src/index.ts` — public entry, high fan-out\n\nPrefer additive changes over restructuring in these files. New tests must include\n  real `expect(...)` assertions — setup-only tests are not accepted.\n- Do not introduce `Date.now()` / `new Date()` in domain code; inject a\n  clock instead.\n- Public API shape is defined by `src/index.ts` exports — breaking\n  changes require a changeset.\n\n## Safety rules\n\n- Don't edit `assets/cjs_shims.js` or other generated/asset files.\n- Don't run `pnpm publish` or push tags.\n- Don't bypass pre-commit hooks.\n```\n\nThat covers the four evidence items in the finding (commands, conventions, load-bearing surface, safety) and would drop `crime_00009` on the next scan. /Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.4/claude/refactor-04-monorepo.json:7:  "response": "# Refactor plan: tighten names, options, and return shapes in `src/index.ts`, `src/rollup.ts`, `src/esbuild/svelte.ts`\n\nFiltering the 79 findings to just the three target files and the three \"petty agent-risk\" charges yields 8 findings across three buckets. Grouped:\n\n---\n\n## 1. `name_behavior_mismatch` (False Identity) — 3 findings\n\n| ID | File:lines | Symbol | Side-effect signals |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| crime_00032 | `src/index.ts:167-459` | `build` | `createLogger`, `removeFiles` (4 signals) |\n| crime_00049 | `src/rollup.ts:38-159` | `getRollupConfig` | `setSilent`, `removeFiles` (4 signals) |\n| crime_00054 | `src/index.ts:291-365` | `buildAll` | `removeFiles` (3 signals) |\n| crime_00072 | `src/rollup.ts:77-81` | `buildStart` | `removeFiles` (2 signals) |\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** A `get*` prefix is the universal \"this is a pure read\" convention. An agent extending `getRollupConfig` to add a new format will reasonably assume it can be called twice, called speculatively, or moved earlier in the boot sequence — and then it silently wipes the user's `outDir` (via `removeFiles`) and flips the logger into silent mode. Same trap for `build`/`buildAll`: the names sound like calculations, but they reach for the filesystem and the logger before doing any work.\n\n**Refactor proposals.**\n- `getRollupConfig` → split into `computeRollupConfig(options)` (pure, returns a config object) and `prepareRollupRun(options)` (does the `setSilent` + `removeFiles`, then calls `computeRollupConfig`). The callers in `src/rollup.ts` and `src/index.ts` migrate to whichever they actually need.\n- `buildStart` (the Rollup plugin hook) — this one is a forced rename only at the boundary: leave the hook itself, but pull `removeFiles` out into an explicit `cleanOutDir(...)` helper called from `prepareRollupRun`, so the hook becomes a one-liner that doesn't surprise.\n- `build` in `src/index.ts` is the public entry point of the package, so renaming is breaking. Instead: extract `runBuild` (does I/O) and keep `build` as a thin wrapper whose JSDoc explicitly documents the side effects (`removes files in outDir`, `creates a logger`). Then the False Identity finding goes away for the inner function, and the outer one has a contract written down.\n- `buildAll` → `runAllBuilds`. Internal, no callers outside this file (cross-check with fan-in finding crime_00046 — `src/index.ts` is fan-in 4, so a quick grep before renaming).\n\n---\n\n## 2. `option_bag_junk_drawer` — 1 finding in scope\n\n| ID | File:lines | Symbol | Property reads |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| crime_00053 | `src/rollup.ts:38-159` | `getRollupConfig(options)` | 10 fields: `cjsInterop, clean, dts, entry, external, format, outDir, outExtension, …` |\n\n(Two more `option_bag_junk_drawer` findings exist in `src/cli-main.ts` and `src/esbuild/index.ts` — out of scope for this task but worth flagging as the same shape.)\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** `options: any`-style bags mean the agent has to read the whole 122-line body to learn which fields are required vs optional vs ignored. They'll often **silently drop** an unrecognized field they meant to add (no compile error, no runtime error, just a quiet no-op), or pass through a `format` value the function doesn't actually support. The fact that this function shares a name and 7 of 10 fields with `runEsbuild`'s options bag (crime_00050) makes it worse — agents will assume they're interchangeable.\n\n**Refactor proposal.**\n- Define a named `RollupBuildOptions` interface (probably in `src/options.ts` or wherever `NormalizedOptions` lives) listing exactly the 10 fields read here, each typed and JSDoc'd. Destructure at the top: `const { cjsInterop, clean, dts, entry, external, format, outDir, outExtension, … } = options;`. That alone moves the contract from \"buried in 122 lines\" to \"visible in the signature.\"\n- If `RollupBuildOptions` turns out to be a strict subset of the package-wide `NormalizedOptions`, use `Pick<NormalizedOptions, …>` rather than a duplicate type — keeps the source of truth singular.\n\n---\n\n## 3. `return_shape_roulette` — 3 findings, all in `src/esbuild/svelte.ts`\n\n| ID | Symbol | Shapes | Example divergence |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| crime_00051 | `sveltePlugin` (9-124) | 6 | `{ args, namespace, path }` vs `{ location, text }` |\n| crime_00052 | `setup` (17-122) | 6 | (same — `setup` is the body of `sveltePlugin`) |\n| crime_00055 | `<anonymous>` (31-121) | 5 | `{ location, text }` vs `{ code }` |\n\nThese are nested: `sveltePlugin` wraps `setup`, which contains the anonymous esbuild `onLoad`/`onResolve` callbacks. The three findings are really one structural problem reported at three nesting levels.\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** Esbuild plugin callbacks have **strict, documented contracts** — `onResolve` returns `{ path, namespace, … } | null`, `onLoad` returns `{ contents, loader, … } | null`, error messages return `{ location, text }`. Today none of those branches have an explicit return type, so an agent adding a new code path (say, handling `.svelte.md`) can return `{ path, contents }` — a hybrid that compiles, satisfies inference, and then crashes inside esbuild with an unhelpful error. The 0% key overlap noted in the evidence is the giveaway: these aren't variants of one type, they're distinct contracts being smuggled through `any`.\n\n**Refactor proposal.**\n- Import the actual esbuild types: `import type { OnResolveResult, OnLoadResult, PartialMessage } from 'esbuild'`.\n- Annotate each callback's return type explicitly:\n  ```ts\n  build.onResolve({ filter: /\\.svelte$/ }, (args): OnResolveResult => { … });\n  build.onLoad({ filter: /\\.svelte$/, namespace: 'svelte' }, (args): OnLoadResult => { … });\n  ```\n  This is a 3-line change per callback that turns silent shape errors into compile errors.\n- Error-object returns (`{ location, text }`) should be typed as `PartialMessage[]` inside the `errors`/`warnings` arrays of `OnLoadResult`, not returned as a top-level shape — fixing the typing will likely expose one or two spots where errors are being returned at the wrong level.\n\n---\n\n## Suggested commit order\n\n1. **svelte.ts return types** (lowest risk, mechanical, kills 3 findings at once).\n2. **`RollupBuildOptions` named type** for `getRollupConfig` — additive, safe.\n3. **Rename `buildAll` → `runAllBuilds`** internally.\n4. **Extract `cleanOutDir`** from `getRollupConfig` / `buildStart` / `build` — once that's a named helper, the False Identity verdict on the surrounding functions weakens naturally because the side effect is now disclosed at the call site.\n5. **Leave the public `build` name alone**; document side effects via JSDoc + extract `runBuild` inner function.\n\nEach step is a separate commit with the corresponding crimes finding IDs in the message, so the next `crimes scan` cleanly shows which ones moved.",

/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.4/claude/bugfix-04-weak-tests.json:7:  "response": "The test I'd strengthen first is **crime_00026** in `test/index.test.ts:537-548` — `\"native-node-module plugin should handle *.node(.js) import properly\"`. It's the only weak-test finding that names an esbuild plugin directly, so it's the most likely path for an esbuild plugin regression to slip through CI.\n\nThe evidence (quoted verbatim):\n\n> - \"test: \\\"native-node-module plugin should handle *.node(.js) import properly\\\"\"\n> - \"0 expect/assert calls\"\n> - \"lines 537-548\"\n\nA test with **0 expect/assert calls** that names a specific plugin and a specific import shape (`*.node(.js)`) is the worst case: it runs the bundler, throws nothing, and passes — which is exactly the \"CI green but regression shipped\" signature the user described.\n\n### The assertion I'd add\n\nThe test name encodes the contract: the native-node-module plugin must \"handle `*.node(.js)` import properly.\" That means two observable behaviors worth pinning:\n\n1. **The build succeeds** with a `.node` (and `.node.js`) import in the entry — `expect(getResult.outFiles).toContain(...)` or `expect(() => run(...)).not.toThrow()`.\n2. **The emitted bundle preserves the native import as an external `require(\"…​.node\")`** rather than trying to inline binary bytes. Concretely, read the output file and assert:\n\n   ```ts\n   const out = await fs.readFile(outFile, 'utf8')\n   expect(out).toMatch(/require\\([\"'][^\"']+\\.node[\"']\\)/)\n   expect(out).not.toMatch(/\\\\x00|Buffer\\.from\\([\"'][A-Za-z0-9+/=]+[\"'], ?[\"']base64[\"']\\)/)\n   ```\n\nThe first matcher pins the intended behavior (the plugin rewrites the import to a runtime `require` of the `.node` file). The second matcher is the regression guard — if the plugin ever stops matching `.node(.js)` and falls back to esbuild's default loader, the binary will get inlined as base64/null bytes and this assertion fails loudly instead of silently passing.\n\nIf I could only add one line, it'd be the `require\\(\"…​.node\"\\)` regex match — that's the single behavior that distinguishes \"plugin worked\" from \"plugin silently no-op'd.\"",
/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.8/claude/refactor-04-monorepo.json:7:  "response": "# Refactor proposal: src/index.ts, src/rollup.ts, src/esbuild/svelte.ts\n\nFive findings cluster across those three files, in three families. None are dangerous individually; together they form the texture that lets an agent walk in and confidently break things.\n\n---\n\n## 1. False Identity (`name_behavior_mismatch`) — 4 findings\n\n| Symbol | File | Lines | What the name promises | What it actually does |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| `build` | src/index.ts | 167–459 | a pure assembly step | `createLogger`, `removeFiles` (+2 more side-effect signals) |\n| `buildAll` | src/index.ts | 291–365 | iterate and assemble | `removeFiles` |\n| `getRollupConfig` | src/rollup.ts | 38–159 | a read — should be memoizable | `setSilent`, `removeFiles` (+2 more) |\n| `buildStart` | src/rollup.ts | 77–81 | lifecycle hook, observe-only | `removeFiles` |\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** The `get`/`build`/`buildStart` prefixes signal *read* or *prepare*. An agent adding a feature is likely to call `getRollupConfig(opts)` twice — once to inspect format, once for the actual run — and wipe the user's `outDir` twice. Or it'll reach for `buildStart` to add a metrics hook, not realizing it's where deletion happens; refactoring its return signature will move the `removeFiles` call to the wrong moment in the lifecycle.\n\n**Refactor.** Separate the pure shape from the effect. For `getRollupConfig`: split into `resolveRollupConfig(opts): RollupConfigInput` (pure) plus `prepareRollupRun(opts)` that calls the resolver and then performs the cleanup/silencing. Same shape for `build` → `prepareBuild` / `runBuild`; `buildStart` → `cleanOutputs` (called from the lifecycle hook explicitly). Names should let a reader predict whether a call is repeatable and whether it touches disk.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Option Bag Junk Drawer (`option_bag_junk_drawer`) — 1 finding in scope\n\n| Symbol | File | Bag | Fields read |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| `getRollupConfig` | src/rollup.ts | `options` | 10 distinct: `cjsInterop, clean, dts, entry, external, format, outDir, outExtension, …` |\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** Nothing in the signature tells the agent which fields are required, which are nested (`dts` is itself an object), which feed Rollup directly vs configure tsup's wrapper, or which combinations are mutually exclusive (`outExtension` interacts with `format` and `cjsInterop`). An agent extending Rollup output handling will guess — read `options.dts` as a boolean when it's `{ entry, resolve, … }`, or add a new field expecting it to be honored downstream when nothing reads it.\n\n**Refactor.** Define a `NormalizedRollupOptions` interface (or zod schema if it's exposed at a boundary) listing exactly the 10 fields the function consumes, with their nested shapes. Destructure at the top of the function:\n\n```ts\nconst { cjsInterop, clean, dts, entry, external, format, outDir, outExtension, … } = options;\n```\n\nThat single change moves the implicit contract into the type system and gives the IDE/agent a discoverable answer to \"what does this function actually need?\"\n\n---\n\n## 3. Return Shape Roulette (`return_shape_roulette`) — 3 findings, all in src/esbuild/svelte.ts\n\n| Symbol | Lines | Shapes | Example divergence |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| `sveltePlugin` | 9–124 | 6 | `{ args, namespace, path }` vs `{ location, text }` |\n| `setup` | 17–122 | 6 | same |\n| inner anonymous | 31–121 | 5 | `{ location, text }` vs `{ code }` |\n\n**What's actually going on.** These aren't one function returning six shapes — they're a stack of esbuild plugin callbacks (`onResolve`, `onLoad`, error reporters) nested inside `setup`, each with a *different* esbuild-defined return contract. The detector reads them as one big arrow because they're inline.\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** An agent asked to \"fix the svelte plugin to support a new file extension\" sees one 91-line arrow and assumes the return shape is fungible. It returns `{ contents, loader }` from what is actually an `onResolve` branch (which wants `{ path, namespace }`), and esbuild silently routes the wrong way — the build fails far from the edit, or worse, succeeds with wrong output.\n\n**Refactor.** Two moves, in order:\n\n1. **Annotate.** Import esbuild's plugin types and annotate each callback: `build.onResolve(filter, (args): OnResolveResult => { … })`, `build.onLoad(filter, (args): OnLoadResult => { … })`. The compiler will immediately reject cross-contract returns.\n2. **Extract each callback to a named top-level helper** (`resolveSvelteImport`, `loadSvelteFile`, `formatCompileError`) with explicit return types. Pass the esbuild `PluginBuild` in. The plugin factory becomes ~15 lines that wire callbacks to filters — readable at a glance.\n\n---\n\n## Cross-cutting note\n\nAll five findings share one root cause: **types and names absorb work that comments and convention currently do**. The fix isn't extraction for its own sake — it's making the type system describe contracts an agent currently has to infer from 100+ line bodies. /Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.5/claude/plan-04-hotspots.json:7:  "response": "# Triage plan — `04-monorepo` hotspots\n\nTwo axes drive the order: **fan-in** (how many places break if you change the file's surface) and **internal complexity** (how much an in-file refactor pays off). Low fan-in + big function = cheap, high-value cleanup. High fan-in + utility surface = leave alone, change only additively.\n\n## Refactor first\n\n**1. `src/esbuild/index.ts` — `runEsbuild` (lines 61–297, 237 lines)**\nFan-in **1**, fan-out 11. Almost nothing imports this file, so extracting helpers inside `runEsbuild` won't ripple — the high fan-out just means the function pulls in many deps, not that callers depend on its shape. `runEsbuild` is 4.0× the domain threshold and also flagged as an Option Bag Junk Drawer (38 distinct `options` reads). Split into named sub-steps + a typed options shape; this is the highest payoff / lowest risk move in the repo.\n\n**2. `src/cli-main.ts` — `main` (lines 11–163, 153 lines)**\nBlast radius **0.04** (CLI entry, nothing imports it). 2.5× threshold and another Option Bag offender. Extract argv parsing, config resolution, and the run dispatch into named functions. Zero ripple risk.\n\n**3. `src/esbuild/postcss.ts` — `postcssPlugin` / `setup` (140 / 126 lines)**\nTwo nested god functions in a plugin file. Plugin files have small surfaces (returned plugin object), so internal extraction is contained. Also has Return Shape Roulette (3 conflicting object shapes with 0% key overlap) — fix the return types while you're in there.\n\n## Refactor carefully (load-bearing but worth it)\n\n**4. `src/index.ts` — `build` (lines 167–459, 293 lines) and `mainTasks` (194 lines)**\nFan-in **4**, fan-out **17**. The file is 459 lines with 28 top-level functions. The four importers care about the exported `build` *signature*, not its internals — so **extract helpers inside the file first** (cheap, no ripple), and only consider splitting the file as a follow-up. Also flagged False Identity (`build` does side effects despite the pure-sounding name): rename or extract the side-effecting parts as part of the same pass. Don't change the export shape in this PR.\n\n## Leave alone (high fan-in — additive changes only)\n\n**5. `src/utils.ts` — fan-in 18, 424 lines, 33 top-level functions**\nEighteen files import this. The God File finding is real but **splitting it is a separate, scoped project** — any rename or move breaks 18 importers. Treat as load-bearing. If individual helpers need work, edit them in place; don't restructure the module.\n\n**6. `src/plugin.ts` — fan-in 10, `buildFinished` (103 lines)**\nTen importers and blast_radius 0.58. The 103-line `buildFinished` is tempting but the agent_risk score (0.66) is driven by the connectivity, not the size. Refactor only if there's a concrete bug to fix; otherwise the cost of breaking 10 callers outweighs the cleanup win.\n\n**7. `src/log.ts` — fan-in 9, fan-out 0**\nPure logging helper, 9 importers. Don't touch the signatures; logging breakage is silent and ripples everywhere.\n\n## Don't bother\n\n**`src/rollup.ts` (fan-in 0, fan-out 7)** and **`src/api-extractor.ts` (fan-in 1, fan-out 6)** — both barely cross the p95 fan-out cutoff. Their large functions (`getRollupConfig` 122 lines, `rollupDtsFiles` 85 lines) are worth a cleanup *if you're already editing them*, but neither earns dedicated work — low connectivity, low blast radius, no current bug signal. **`test/utils.ts`** is test infrastructure; the fan-in 9 is expected.\n\n## Summary order\n\n| # | File | Why first/last |\n|---|---|---|\n| 1 | `src/esbuild/index.ts` | fan-in 1 → free to restructure; biggest function in the repo |\n| 2 | `src/cli-main.ts` | blast_radius 0.04; pure entry-point cleanup |\n| 3 | `src/esbuild/postcss.ts` | self-contained plugin; fix return shapes at the same time |\n| 4 | `src/index.ts` (internal only) | extract within the file; do **not** change exports |\n| — | `src/utils.ts`, `src/plugin.ts`, `src/log.ts` | fan-in 9–18; additive changes only |\n| — | `src/rollup.ts`, `src/api-extractor.ts` | borderline hotspots, no current pain |",
/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.5/claude/context-04-monorepo.json:7:  "response": "## Finding: `crime_00011` — Missing Agent Context (medium, conf 0.9, agent_risk 0.61)\n\nThe scan confirms the repo ships **no agent-readable instruction file**:\n\n- no `AGENTS.md` at repo root\n- no `CLAUDE.md` at repo root\n- no `.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md`\n- `package.json` declares bins (`tsup`, `tsup-node`) with no discoverable doc surface\n\n## Consequence for future agent work\n\nAn agent landing in this repo cold has to **infer** everything: which command builds (`tsup` vs `tsc`?), which tests to run, what the package layout means (`src/esbuild/`, `src/rollup.ts`, `src/api-extractor.ts` are all separate build paths inside one tool), and what's load-bearing. The same scan flags `src/plugin.ts` (fan-in 10), `src/utils.ts` (fan-in 18), and `src/index.ts` (459-line god file with a 293-line `build` function) — all places where a confident-but-wrong edit is easy without a map. Result: more wasted turns, more risk of touching the wrong layer (the boundary between `esbuild/`, `rollup.ts`, and `tsc.ts` is non-obvious from filenames alone).\n\n## Minimal `AGENTS.md` skeleton (clears the finding)\n\n```markdown\n# AGENTS.md\n\n## What this is\ntsup — a TypeScript/JavaScript bundler powered by esbuild, with Rollup\nused only for `.d.ts` generation. Single package, not a monorepo.\n\n## Commands\n- Install:  `pnpm install`\n- Build:    `pnpm build`        (runs tsup on itself)\n- Test:     `pnpm test`         (vitest, in `test/`)\n- Lint:     `pnpm lint`\n- Typecheck: `pnpm typecheck`\n\nAlways run `pnpm test` before declaring a change done.\n\n## Layout\n- `src/index.ts`         — public API entry (`build()`); orchestrates a run\n- `src/cli-main.ts`      — CLI argument parsing and dispatch\n- `src/esbuild/`         — esbuild pipeline + plugins (postcss, svelte, swc)\n- `src/rollup.ts`        — Rollup pipeline used only for `.d.ts` rollups\n- `src/api-extractor.ts` — alternative `.d.ts` path via API Extractor\n- `src/tsc.ts`           — `.d.ts` emit via `tsc`\n- `src/plugin.ts`        — internal plugin interface (high fan-in; edit carefully)\n- `src/utils.ts`         — shared helpers (high fan-in; additive changes preferred)\n- `test/`                — vitest specs; fixtures under `test/fixtures/`\n\n## Conventions\n- TypeScript strict mode; no `any` in new code.\n- Public API changes go through `src/index.ts` and need a changeset\n  (`pnpm changeset`).\n- The three `.d.ts` paths (`rollup.ts`, `api-extractor.ts`, `tsc.ts`)\n  are alternatives selected by user config — don't merge them.\n\n## Load-bearing files (read before editing)\n- `src/plugin.ts`, `src/utils.ts`, `src/index.ts`, `src/log.ts`\n  Prefer additive edits; restructuring ripples widely.\n\n## Safety\n- Don't introduce `Date.now()` / `new Date()` in domain code — inject a\n  clock. Existing instances in `api-extractor.ts`, `esbuild/index.ts`,\n  `rollup.ts`, `tsc.ts` are timing logs and should also be migrated.\n- Don't commit changes to `dist/` or `assets/cjs_shims.js`.\n```\n\nThat's ~40 lines and would flip `crime_00011` from medium to absent on the next scan. /Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.5/claude/refactor-04-monorepo.json:7:  "response": "# Refactor plan — three files, three finding types\n\nI'll work straight from the JSON in the prompt rather than re-scanning. The \"petty\" cluster across `src/index.ts`, `src/rollup.ts`, and `src/esbuild/svelte.ts` breaks down into three finding types. Each section names the offenders, explains the agent trap, and proposes a concrete fix.\n\n---\n\n## 1. False Identity (`name_behavior_mismatch`)\n\n| Finding | Symbol | Side-effect evidence |\n|---|---|---|\n| crime_00032 | `src/index.ts:build` (167–459) | `createLogger`, `removeFiles`, 4 side-effect signals |\n| crime_00054 | `src/index.ts:buildAll` (291–365) | `removeFiles`, 3 side-effect signals |\n| crime_00049 | `src/rollup.ts:getRollupConfig` (38–159) | `setSilent`, `removeFiles`, 4 side-effect signals |\n| crime_00072 | `src/rollup.ts:buildStart` (77–81) | `removeFiles`, 2 side-effect signals |\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** All four names imply pure reads (`build` reads like a noun-builder/factory, `getRollupConfig` reads like a getter, `buildStart` reads like a lifecycle no-op). An agent extending this code is likely to:\n\n- Memoize `getRollupConfig` to avoid \"recomputing config\" — silently dropping the `clean`/`setSilent` calls on cache hits.\n- Call `build()` twice in a watcher loop (e.g. \"compute first, run later\") — deleting files twice.\n- Wrap `buildStart` in a debounce — turning a clean-before-build into an intermittent clean.\n\n**Proposal.**\n\n- **`build` → `runBuild`.** Extract a pure `resolveBuildPlan(options): BuildPlan` step; have `runBuild` accept that plan and own the logger/`removeFiles` side effects. This also cuts the 293-line God Function (crime_00003) into separable units.\n- **`buildAll` → `runAllBuilds`.** Hoist the `removeFiles` call to the caller (or guard it behind a one-shot flag) so the per-build function is genuinely side-effect-free.\n- **`getRollupConfig` → split.** Rename to `buildRollupConfig` (or `prepareRollupBuild`) and extract two helpers: `applyLogLevel(opts)` and `cleanOutDirs(opts)`. The function is currently a getter that destroys filesystem state — that's the heart of the agent risk.\n- **`buildStart`.** The name is fixed by Rollup's plugin contract, so don't rename. Instead, gate the `removeFiles` call behind `if (options.clean)` and add a one-line comment naming the side effect — that's the minimum disclosure.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Option Bag Junk Drawer (`option_bag_junk_drawer`)\n\n| Finding | Symbol | Property reads |\n|---|---|---|\n| crime_00053 | `src/rollup.ts:getRollupConfig` | 10: `cjsInterop, clean, dts, entry, external, format, outDir, outExtension, …` |\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** `getRollupConfig(options)` accepts the full `NormalizedOptions` and pulls 10 fields out of it implicitly. An agent that:\n\n- Adds a new option (`outDirs` plural, `dtsEntry` separate) won't know whether to wire it here — the function reads the bag opaquely.\n- Removes a field they grep-confirmed is \"only read in `index.ts`\" can silently break this codepath because the property access is hidden inside method bodies.\n- Tries to reuse `getRollupConfig` from a test by passing a `{}` literal — TypeScript accepts it (the param is the wide `NormalizedOptions`), runtime fails on the first missing field.\n\n**Proposal.** Replace `options: NormalizedOptions` with an explicit, narrower shape:\n\n```ts\ntype RollupConfigInputs = Pick<\n  NormalizedOptions,\n  'cjsInterop' | 'clean' | 'dts' | 'entry' | 'external'\n  | 'format' | 'outDir' | 'outExtension' | 'silent' | /* …the remaining 1 */\n>;\n```\n\nEven better: destructure at the call site so each helper takes only what it needs. That converts the agent's question from \"does this function use field X?\" to a type error.\n\nThe same pattern applies to crime_00050 (`runEsbuild` reads 38 distinct properties) and crime_00056/57 (`main` reads 8) — the user scoped the question to the three named files, but the same `Pick<>`/destructure recipe lifts straight over.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Return Shape Roulette (`return_shape_roulette`)\n\n| Finding | Symbol | Shapes | Example divergence |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| crime_00051 | `src/esbuild/svelte.ts:sveltePlugin` (9–124) | 6, 0% overlap | `{ args, namespace, path }` vs `{ location, text }` |\n| crime_00052 | `src/esbuild/svelte.ts:setup` (17–122) | 6, 0% overlap | same — `setup` is the inner callback of `sveltePlugin` |\n| crime_00055 | `src/esbuild/svelte.ts:<anonymous>` (31–121) | 5, 0% overlap | `{ location, text }` vs `{ code }` |\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today.** Without an annotated return type, TS infers the union `{args,namespace,path} | {location,text} | {code} | …`. The shapes are esbuild plugin callbacks (`onResolve` → `OnResolveResult`, `onLoad` → `OnLoadResult`, errors → `PartialMessage`, transform → `{ code }`), but the file doesn't *say* that. An agent who:\n\n- Adds an error path returning `{ message: 'svelte preprocess failed' }` gets no TS error — esbuild silently ignores the unknown shape and the diagnostic disappears.\n- Refactors one branch into a helper and lifts the inferred return type as the helper's signature — now every caller pays for the union and the next agent assumes all branches return all fields.\n- Tries to add a `pluginData` field to the resolve result by spreading it onto the error-path return.\n\n**Proposal.** Annotate each callback explicitly with esbuild's exported types:\n\n```ts\nimport type {\n  OnResolveResult, OnLoadResult, PartialMessage,\n} from 'esbuild';\n\nbuild.onResolve({ filter }, async (args): Promise<OnResolveResult | undefined> => { … });\nbuild.onLoad({ filter }, async (args): Promise<OnLoadResult> => { … });\n```\n\nThat single change converts all three findings on this file: future divergent shapes fail at the type checker instead of at runtime, and an agent reading the file sees the contract without having to infer it from six return statements.\n\n---\n\n## Order of operations\n\n1. **Group 3 first (svelte.ts annotations).** Pure type additions, zero behavior change, removes three findings.\n2. **Group 1 renames + side-effect extraction.** Touches more files but each rename is mechanical; the `getRollupConfig` split also chips at the God Function in crime_00008.\n3. **Group 2 (`RollupConfigInputs`).** Do last — narrowing the type may surface bugs in the call sites that the prior two steps haven't already touched.\n\nNone of these are load-bearing in the high_fan_in_fan_out sense for the three target files (`rollup.ts` fan-in 0, `svelte.ts` not in the connectivity tier, `index.ts` is fan-in 4 but the renames are internal). Safe to do without an importer survey.",

Every other weak-signal finding either targets dts/tsconfig/shims/graphql flows (`crime_00018`–`crime_00025`, `crime_00027`–`crime_00029`) or uses weak matchers on non-plugin behaviour (`crime_00058`–`crime_00071`) — none of those guard the esbuild plugin path.\n\n**Assertion to add:** after the build runs, read the emitted bundle and assert it contains the rewritten `require(...)` (or `createRequire`) call for the `*.node` / `*.node.js` import, e.g. `expect(output).toMatch(/require\\(['\"].+\\.node['\"]\\)/)` and `expect(output).not.toContain('import ')` for the native module specifier. That pins the observable contract — \"the plugin actually rewrote the native-module import\" — which is precisely what the regression would have broken while the current setup-only test stayed green.",
/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.5/claude/bugfix-01-messy-ts-app.json:7:  "response": "# Most likely cause: `crime_00004` — Temporal Recklessness in `src/billing.ts`\n\nDate-boundary flakiness almost always traces back to domain code reading the wall clock at runtime, and the scan points straight at it: **7 direct `Date.now()` / `new Date()` calls** in `src/billing.ts` at lines 57, 63, 92, 198, 238, 250, 273 — all inside the `generateInvoice` God Function (lines 50–253, `crime_00001`).\n\nThat's the exact signature of a date-boundary bug:\n\n- Tests pass during the day, fail near midnight / month-end / year-end / DST shifts.\n- Each `Date.now()` and `new Date()` call observes a *different* instant — if the function spans a boundary mid-execution (e.g. line 57 reads \"today\" and line 250 reads \"tomorrow\"), invoice math goes off by a day.\n- The weak test signal at `crime_00006` (`\"renders the enterprise plan\"` has 0 assertions) means a whole code path is uncovered, so the bug is only caught when an asserting test happens to straddle the boundary.\n\nSupporting evidence that points the same direction, not away:\n- `crime_00005` shows `src/date.ts` has its own 5 raw clock reads — so there's probably *already* a date module that should be the single source of \"now\", but `billing.ts` is bypassing it.\n- `crime_00010` (God File) and `crime_00001` (God Function) explain why nobody caught it: 204 lines of mixed responsibility, and an agent editing the discount section won't load the proration section into context.\n\n# Proposed fix\n\n**1. Inject a clock — don't read wall time in domain code.**\n\n```ts\n// src/date.ts (or a new src/clock.ts)\nexport type Clock = { now: () => Date };\nexport const systemClock: Clock = { now: () => new Date() };\n```\n\n**2. Thread it through `generateInvoice`** (and anything it calls). Take a single `now: Date` *once* at the top of the function and use it everywhere — this also fixes the mid-function boundary-crossing bug, even if a test forgets to inject:\n\n```ts\nexport function generateInvoice(input: InvoiceInput, clock: Clock = systemClock) {\n  const now = clock.now();          // pin the instant once\n  // ...replace all 7 Date.now()/new Date() sites with `now` or derivations of it\n}\n```\n\n**3. Add the failing-boundary test** with a pinned clock — UTC midnight, last day of month, DST forward, leap day:\n\n```ts\nconst fixedClock: Clock = { now: () => new Date('2026-02-28T23:59:59.999Z') };\nexpect(generateInvoice(input, fixedClock).billingPeriod).toBe('2026-02');\n```\n\n**4. While you're there, fix `crime_00006`** — the assertion-free `\"renders the enterprise plan\"` test. That's the test that *should* have been catching this and isn't.\n\n# What to defer\n\nSplitting the God Function (`crime_00001`), splitting the God File (`crime_00010`), and centralising `\"enterprise\"` (`crime_00007`) are all worth doing — but not in the same change as the bugfix. Land the clock injection + boundary test first so the regression is locked down, then refactor on green.",
/Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.15/claude/plan-04-hotspots.json:7:  "response": "## Triage order\n\nReasoning: the goal is to *reduce blast radius across the codebase*, so I'm ranking by **how many other files depend on the change site** (fan-in), then by **how much risk the body itself concentrates** (length × agent_risk). /Users/andrew/dev/crimes-release-a/evals/results/0.7.15/claude/refactor-04-monorepo.json:7:  "response": "# Refactor plan: tightening types and names in the three petty-crime hotspots\n\nFocusing on the petty agent-risk findings (`name_behavior_mismatch`, `option_bag_junk_drawer`, `return_shape_roulette`) scoped to `src/index.ts`, `src/rollup.ts`, and `src/esbuild/svelte.ts`. God-function and direct-Date findings in these same files are out of scope per the scenario — they're symptoms of a deeper split, not the \"tighten types and clarify names\" target.\n\n## 1. False Identity — names that lie about side effects\n\n| ID | Symbol | File:lines | Reads as | Actually does |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| crime_00030 | `build` | `src/index.ts:167–459` | pure transform | `createLogger`, `removeFiles`, 4 side-effect signals |\n| crime_00052 | `getRollupConfig` | `src/rollup.ts:38–159` | read (`get*`) | `setSilent`, `removeFiles` mutate global logger/disk |\n| crime_00079 | `buildStart` | `src/rollup.ts:77–81` | pure rollup hook body | calls `removeFiles` |\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today:** the prefixes `build*`, `get*`, `buildStart` all signal \"safe to call twice / safe to memoize / no I/O\". An agent adding a retry loop around `build`, caching `getRollupConfig`'s result, or moving `buildStart` earlier in a watch loop has no static signal that disk-clean and global-mutex flips happen inside.\n\n**Proposal:**\n- `build(options) → runBuild(options)` — the verb already exists in `mainTasks` / `buildAll`; switch to `run*` consistently for the \"this performs I/O\" tier.\n- `getRollupConfig(options) → resolveRollupConfig(options)` and **extract** the `removeFiles` + `setSilent` calls into an explicit `prepareRollupSession(options)` step called by the orchestrator. The pure config-shape derivation can stay in `resolveRollupConfig` with an explicit return type.\n- `buildStart` is a rollup plugin hook name — keep it, but move `removeFiles` out into the `prepareRollupSession` step above so the hook body becomes a no-op or a logger nudge.\n\nNet: the prefix tells the truth, and the two side effects (`clean` + `silence`) become one named lifecycle call you can find with grep.\n\n## 2. Option Bag Junk Drawer — `options` parameters with implicit shapes\n\n| ID | Symbol | File | Distinct reads |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| crime_00056 | `getRollupConfig` | `src/rollup.ts:38–159` | 10 (`cjsInterop`, `clean`, `dts`, `entry`, `external`, `format`, `outDir`, `outExtension`, …) |\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today:** `options` is typed broadly (likely the union `NormalizedOptions` or `Options`), so reading a field that doesn't exist on the relevant variant compiles. An agent adding `options.someNewFlag` has no signal that callers have actually populated it; an agent adding a required field to the type silently breaks every call site that didn't go through `normalizeOptions`.\n\n**Proposal:**\n- Introduce a `RollupConfigInputs` named type with exactly the 10 fields read, derived from `NormalizedOptions` via `Pick<>`. Signature becomes `resolveRollupConfig(inputs: RollupConfigInputs)`.\n- Destructure at the parameter — `({ cjsInterop, clean, dts, entry, … }: RollupConfigInputs)` — so unused fields become a deletion candidate, not silent overpassing.\n- Side benefit: the new type doubles as the contract for the eventual extraction out of `src/index.ts`'s `build`, where these same fields are computed.\n\n(The same pattern applies to `runEsbuild` and `main` in other files — flagging here but explicitly deferred per scenario scope.)\n\n## 3. Return Shape Roulette — `src/esbuild/svelte.ts` returning unrelated shapes\n\n| ID | Symbol | Lines | Shapes | Example keys |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| crime_00054 | `sveltePlugin` | 9–124 | 6, 0% min overlap | `{ args, namespace, path }` vs `{ location, text }` |\n| crime_00055 | `setup` (class method) | 17–122 | 6, 0% min overlap | same |\n| crime_00062 | `<anonymous>` callback | 31–121 | 5, 0% min overlap | `{ location, text }` vs `{ code }` |\n\nThese three are the same function nest: `sveltePlugin` is the factory, `setup` is its body, and the anonymous arrow is the `onLoad` / `onResolve` callback inside `setup`. The 6 shapes are esbuild's plugin protocol — different callbacks return different result types (resolved path vs load result vs error), but the file has no explicit return annotations, so TS infers a union.\n\n**What an agent gets wrong today:** the inferred union widens silently each time you add a branch. An agent adding a new error path that returns `{ message }` instead of `{ text }` won't get a TS error at the return site — they'll only see it when esbuild fails to display the diagnostic at runtime. Worse, the `{ location, text }` shape is esbuild's `PartialMessage` and `{ args, namespace, path }` is `OnResolveResult`; mixing them in one inferred return type means the agent has no signal which callback they're inside.\n\n**Proposal:**\n- Annotate each callback with its esbuild type explicitly: the `onResolve` arrow returns `OnResolveResult | null | undefined`, the `onLoad` arrow returns `OnLoadResult | null | undefined`. These types ship with `esbuild`.\n- Annotate `setup`'s return as `void` (esbuild plugins' `setup` is void-returning by contract).\n- Annotate `sveltePlugin`'s return as `Plugin` from esbuild.\n\nNo structural refactor needed — this is purely \"type the seams\". After this, the inferred-union noise disappears and the detector goes quiet.\n\n## Ordering and risk\n\n1. **First**: the svelte.ts type annotations (lowest risk — pure additions, can ship as one commit).\n2. **Second**: `getRollupConfig → resolveRollupConfig` + `RollupConfigInputs` (one file, one signature, callers compile-checked).\n3. **Third**: extract `prepareRollupSession` and rename `build → runBuild` (touches `src/index.ts`'s `build` orchestrator and `src/rollup.ts`'s `buildStart`; needs a coordinated commit because the side effects move).\n\nNone of these require touching the god-function structure, the `Date.now()` calls, or the test gaps that the larger scan flagged — those are separate workstreams. The scenario's three files share one underlying problem: the boundary between \"compute a config / shape\" and \"do disk and logger I/O\" is invisible to the type system, and an agent has nothing but charity to tell which is which.",

test('native-node-module plugin should handle *.node(.js) import properly', async () => {
  await run(
    getTestName(),
    {
      'input.tsx': `export * from './hi.node'`,
      'hi.node.js': `export const hi = 'hi'`,
    },
    {
      entry: ['input.tsx'],
    },
  )
})

test('proper sourcemap sources path when swc is enabled', async () => {
  const { getFileContent } = await run(
    getTestName(),
    {
      'input.ts': `export const hi = 'hi'`,
      'tsconfig.json': JSON.stringify({
import fs from 'node:fs'
import fsp from 'node:fs/promises'
import path from 'node:path'
import { fileURLToPath } from 'node:url'
import { expect } from 'vitest'
import { exec } from 'tinyexec'
import { glob } from 'tinyglobby'

const __dirname = path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url))
const cacheDir = path.resolve(__dirname, '.cache')
const bin = path.resolve(__dirname, '../dist/cli-default.js')

export function getTestName() {
  const name = expect
    .getState()
    .currentTestName?.replace(/^[a-z]+/g, '_')
    .replace(/-/g, '_')

  if (!name) {
    throw new Error('No test name')
  }

  return name
}

export async function run(
  title: string,
  files: { [name: string]: string },
  options: {
    entry?: string[]
    flags?: string[]
    env?: Record<string, string>
  } = {},
) {
  const testDir = path.resolve(cacheDir, filenamify(title))

  // Write entry files on disk
  await Promise.all(
    Object.keys(files).map(async (name) => {
      const filePath = path.resolve(testDir, name)
      const parentDir = path.dirname(filePath)
      // Thanks to `recursive: true`, this doesn't fail even if the directory already exists.
      await fsp.mkdir(parentDir, { recursive: true })
      return fsp.writeFile(filePath, files[name], 'utf8')
    }),
  )

  const entry = options.entry || ['input.ts']

  // Run tsup cli
  const processPromise = exec(bin, [...entry, ...(options.flags || [])], {
    nodeOptions: {
      cwd: testDir,
      env: { ...process.env, ...options.env },
    },
  })
  const { stdout, stderr } = await processPromise

  const logs = stdout + stderr
  if (processPromise.exitCode !== 0) {
    throw new Error(logs)
  }

  // Get output
  const outFiles = await glob(['**/*'], {
    cwd: path.resolve(testDir, 'dist'),
  }).then((res) => res.sort())

  return {
    get output() {
      return fs.readFileSync(path.resolve(testDir, 'dist/input.js'), 'utf8')
    },
    outFiles,
    logs,
    outDir: path.resolve(testDir, 'dist'),
    getFileContent(filename: string) {
      return fsp.readFile(path.resolve(testDir, filename), 'utf8')
    },
  }
}

function filenamify(input: string) {
  return input.replace(/[^a-zA-Z0-9]/g, '-')
}
